topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat on my mahogany desk like a deliberate insult. I hadn't expected it. Not after all these years, not after the sheer, unadulterated carelessness of that summer. My jaw tightened as I slowly picked up the letter, the paper feeling flimsy beneath my fingers. Inside, the script was neat, formal, and utterly devoid of the remorse I had been anticipating. A carefully worded acknowledgment of past failings. I let out a short, sharp exhale, a sound more like a hiss than a breath. I folded the letter with unnecessary precision, smoothing out every crease until it was perfect. I didn't burn it. Burning felt too dramatic, too messy. Instead, I placed it in a drawer, the metallic click echoing loudly in the sudden silence of the room. A faint, unpleasant warmth bloomed in my chest, a sensation I quickly tried to smother with cold indifference.

[story 2]

When the postman delivered the package, I didn't even look up from the financial reports spread across my antique desk. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the blotter. The weight of the letter felt significant, an unwelcome intrusion into the carefully constructed order of my solitude. I tore it open with a sudden, jerky movement, the sound of the tearing paper surprisingly loud. Reading the contents brought a slow, deliberate tightening in my chest, a pressure that demanded release. I reread the sentences three times, focusing intently on the precise wording, searching for any flaw, any weakness. When I finally dropped the paper onto the floor, it landed with a soft, almost mocking thud. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my movements sharp and agitated. I needed to dispose of this evidence, to erase the very notion that such an event had ever held any weight for me.

[story 3]

It arrived on a Tuesday, utterly unannounced, and I found myself staring at the address label with narrowed eyes. Years had passed, and yet, this correspondence managed to elicit a visceral reaction—a low, simmering irritation that refused to dissipate. I picked up the letter, my fingers brushing against the heavy cardstock. I didn't bother with formalities; I just wanted it gone. I crumpled the paper into a tight, dense ball, the fibers protesting under the pressure. I didn't throw it into the waste bin; that felt too simple. Instead, I walked to the fireplace, the embers glowing faintly, and let the ball fall into the grate. The ensuing small flare of smoke was the only acknowledgment I allowed myself. I watched the ash settle, a small, satisfied smirk playing on my lips as I turned back to my work, the lingering feeling a hard, cold knot beneath my ribs.
